title: GENERAL STANDARD FOR EDIBLE FUNGI AND FUNGUS PRODUCTS

sections.section: GENERAL STANDARD FOR EDIBLE FUNGI AND FUNGUS PRODUCTS
sections.summary: Document title only: General Standard for Edible Fungi and Fungus Products (no substantive provisions in this section).
Heading only — identifies the standard as CXS 38-1981 and contains no substantive provisions.
Administrative history: standard adopted in 1981 and amended in 2022 and 2023.
Notifies that, following CAC decisions at its 45th Session (Dec 2022), amendments were made to Section 8.4 on labelling of non‑retail containers.
Notifies that, following CAC decisions at its 46th Session (Dec 2023), amendments were made to Section 9 on methods of analysis and sampling.

Scope: sets general requirements applicable to all edible fungi (fresh or processed) permitted for sale by competent authorities, except canned cultivated Agaricus mushrooms, and notes that group or individual standards may set different requirements.

Descriptive section defining product types, defect definitions, the scope of permitted species, and requirements for examination and sorting of raw material to ensure only edible species are marketed or processed.

Defines 'edible fungi' and a range of product forms (fresh, mixed, dried, grits, powder, pickled, salted, fermented, quick‑frozen, extracts, concentrates, sterilized, oil‑packed, mycelium cakes), and specifies key processing/format criteria such as powder passing a 200‑micron sieve, quick‑freezing to −18 °C at the thermal centre, and extract/concentrate definitions by percent saltless extract (7% and 24%).

Defines defects and measurement criteria for edible fungi, including 'damaged' (>¼ cap missing), 'crushed' (pieces passing a 15×15 mm sieve for fresh, 5×5 mm for dried), 'spoiled' (brownish/rotten), maggot‑damage (holes; ≥4 holes = serious), and definitions for organic (vegetable) and mineral impurities.
Specifies that the standard covers all edible fungi species permitted for sale by the competent authorities in consuming countries.

sections.section: 2.4 Examination and sorting of raw material
sections.summary: Requires careful selection and sorting so only edible species are collected and marketed, and mandates expert examination and removal of inedible fungi from wild‑harvested lots.

Establishes essential composition and quality factors for fresh fungi and fungus products, including condition/composition requirements, tolerances for defects, permitted ingredients and presentation styles, and numeric quality limits such as water content and impurity/maggot‑damage tolerances for various product types (e.g., specific water‑content limits for dried, grits and powder, and impurity/maggot‑damage percentages and ingredient limits for pickled and fermented products).

Sets out requirements for fresh fungi: acceptable condition (healthy, unspoiled, clean, firm, minimally maggot‑damaged), a composition rule on stalk/cap ratio, and that tolerances for defects differ for wild‑growing and cultivated fungi.

sections.section: 3.1.1 Condition : Fresh edible fungi shall be healthy, i.e. not spoiled, practically clean, firm, undamaged, free, as far as possible, from maggot damage and shall possess the flavour and taste appropriate for the species.
sections.summary: Quality requirement: fresh edible fungi must be healthy (not spoiled), practically clean, firm, undamaged, largely free from maggot damage and possess species‑appropriate flavour.

sections.section: 3.1.2 Composition : The number of stalks shall not exceed the number of caps.
sections.summary: Composition requirement: the number of stalks in fresh fungi shall not exceed the number of caps.
Heading indicating that specific tolerances for defects are prescribed, distinguishing requirements for wild‑growing and cultivated fungi.
For wild‑growing fungi, tolerances are set for mineral impurities, organic (vegetable) impurities and content of maggot‑damaged fungi (specific limits provided elsewhere in the standard).
For cultivated fungi, the standard sets tolerances for mineral impurities, organic impurities (including compost material) and maggot damage, with distinctions made between uncut and cut forms.

General requirements for fungus products: raw material must be freshly processed to avoid deterioration and be healthy/clean/undamaged, permitted ingredients are listed, multiple presentation styles are allowed subject to meeting relevant requirements and labelling to avoid consumer confusion, and composition rules (e.g., stalks not exceeding caps) apply unless otherwise declared on the label.

sections.section: 3.2.1 Raw material : only fresh edible fungi which have been treated or processed immediately after they have been picked, before deterioration sets in, may be used in the preparation of fungus products.
sections.summary: Raw‑material rule: only fresh edible fungi processed immediately after picking may be used for fungus products and both raw and preserved material must be healthy, clean, undamaged and as far as possible free from maggot damage, with numerical limits for impurities and damage specified in the standard.
Permitted ingredients for fungus products include salt, vinegar, spices and herbs, sugars, refined edible vegetable oil or animal fat, butter, milk and milk products, cream, water and wine.
Lists permitted presentation styles for processed fungi, e.g., whole with stalks, whole caps (buttons) without stalks, slices, pieces and stalks, grits, powder or concentrate.

Permits any other presentation style for the product provided it is sufficiently distinctive, meets all relevant requirements of this standard (including defect limits, drained weight and other requirements applicable to the most similar style), and is clearly described on the label to avoid misleading the consumer.
Requires that, except for products consisting entirely of caps or where addition of stalks is declared on the label per subsection 8.1.6, the number of stalks must not exceed the number of caps.

Contains special requirements for various fungus product types (dried, grits/powder, pickled, fermented, in oil, quick-frozen, sterilized, extracts/concentrates, salted), specifying quality criteria (e.g., colour, flavour, moisture), permitted ingredients (salt, sugars, vinegar, oils), maximum moisture and salt levels, and tolerances for defects (mineral/organic impurities, maggot damage) with numeric limits that vary by product type.

Sets quality criteria for dried fungi including appropriate species-specific colour and flavour and maximum water content limits: freeze-dried 6% m/m, dried (other than freeze-dried) 12% m/m, and dried Shii‑ta‑ke 13% m/m.
Requires colour and flavour appropriate to the species and specifies maximum water content for dried fungi: freeze-dried 6% m/m, dried (other than freeze-dried) 12% m/m, and dried Shii‑ta‑ke 13% m/m.
Identifies permitted defects for dried fungi as mineral impurities, organic impurities of vegetable origin, and presence of maggot‑damaged fungi (distinguishing wild‑growing and cultivated sources).
Defines quality criteria for fungus grits and powders including maximum water content and various impurity and damage limits, with some species-specific exceptions referenced (e.g., Shii‑ta‑ke).

Specifies water content maxima for fungus grits (not more than 13% m/m) and fungus powder (not more than 9% m/m) and additional small‑quantity impurity and damage thresholds including very low impurity limits (e.g., 0.02% m/m) and total/serious damage limits with species exceptions.

For quick‑frozen fungi sets tolerances for defects: mineral impurities not more than 0.2% m/m, organic vegetable‑origin impurities not more than 0.02% m/m, maggot‑damaged content for wild‑growing fungi not more than 6% m/m total including not more than 2% m/m serious damage, and for cultivated fungi not more than 1% m/m total including not more than 0.5% m/m serious damage.

Specifies tolerances for defects in quick‑frozen fungi: mineral impurities ≤0.2% m/m, organic vegetable impurities ≤0.02% m/m, maggot‑damage limits of ≤6% m/m total (≤2% m/m serious) for wild‑growing and ≤1% m/m total (≤0.5% m/m serious) for cultivated fungi.

sections.section: Content of maggot-damaged fungi: wild-growing fungi
sections.summary: Sets maximum maggot‑damaged content for wild‑growing fungi at not more than 6% m/m total damage, including not more than 2% m/m serious damage.
Sets maximum maggot‑damaged content for cultivated fungi at not more than 1% m/m total damage, including not more than 0.5% m/m serious damage.

sections.section: not more than 1% m/m of total damage including not more than 0.5% m/m serious damage
sections.summary: Numeric limit: maximum total damage for cultivated fungi is not more than 1% m/m, including a maximum of 0.5% m/m serious damage.

For sterilized fungi permits salt as an ingredient (limit given) and sets tolerances for defects: mineral impurities not more than 0.2% m/m, organic vegetable impurities not more than 0.02% m/m, and maggot‑damage limits (wild ≤6% total/≤2% serious; cultivated ≤1% total/≤0.5% serious).
Permits salt (sodium chloride) as an ingredient for sterilized fungi with a quantitative limit specified elsewhere in the text.
Non‑substantive fragment listing 'Salt (sodium chloride)' as an ingredient.
Fragment containing the numeric limit 'not more than 2% m/m' without additional context in this excerpt.

Specifies tolerances for defects (sterilized fungi context): mineral impurities ≤0.2% m/m, organic vegetable‑origin impurities ≤0.02% m/m, and maggot‑damage limits of ≤6% m/m total (≤2% m/m serious) for wild‑growing and ≤1% m/m total (≤0.5% m/m serious) for cultivated fungi.
States for cultivated fungi a maximum total damage of not more than 1% m/m, including not more than 0.5% m/m serious damage.

sections.section: not more than 1% m/m of total damage including not more than 0.5% m/m serious damage
sections.summary: Sets a limit on maggot-damaged fungi content of not more than 1% m/m total damage, including not more than 0.5% m/m classified as serious damage.

For fungus extract and fungus concentrate, permits salt (sodium chloride) up to 20% m/m and lists tolerances for defects including mineral impurities and a prohibition on organic impurities of vegetable origin (none specified); mineral impurity limits are not shown in the excerpt.
Permits salt (sodium chloride) as an ingredient in fungus extract/concentrate at a maximum of 20% m/m.
Names salt (sodium chloride) as an ingredient (permitted substance).
Specifies a maximum level of 20% m/m (context indicates this applies to salt content).

Tolerances for defects in fungus extract/concentrate list mineral impurities and state that organic impurities of vegetable origin are not permitted (none), with no numeric mineral-impurity limit shown in the excerpt.

For dried fungus concentrate, sets a moisture limit of not more than 9% m/m, permits salt (sodium chloride) up to 5% m/m, and disallows mineral impurities and organic impurities of vegetable origin (none permitted).
Sets the water (moisture) content quality criterion for dried fungus concentrate at not more than 9% m/m.
Permits salt (sodium chloride) as an ingredient in dried fungus concentrate at a maximum of 5% m/m.
Names salt (sodium chloride) as an ingredient (permitted substance).
Specifies a maximum level of 5% m/m (context indicates this applies to salt content).

States that permitted defects for dried fungus concentrate include mineral impurities and organic impurities of vegetable origin and indicates none are permitted (no mineral-impurity numeric limits shown).

For salted fungi (semi-processed), requires salt content between not less than 15% m/m and not more than 18% m/m, limits mineral impurities to not more than 0.3% m/m and organic impurities of vegetable origin to not more than 0.05% m/m, and sets maggot-damage limits of up to 6% m/m total (including up to 2% m/m serious) for wild-gathered fungi and up to 1% m/m total (including up to 0.5% m/m serious) for cultivated fungi.
Permits salt (sodium chloride) in salted fungi at not less than 15% m/m and not more than 18% m/m.
Names salt (sodium chloride) as an ingredient (permitted substance).

sections.section: not less than 15% m/m and not more than 18% m/m
sections.summary: Specifies the required salt range of not less than 15% m/m and not more than 18% m/m.

Lists tolerances for defects in salted fungi: mineral impurities ≤0.3% m/m, organic (vegetable) impurities ≤0.05% m/m, maggot-damage limits of ≤6% m/m total (≤2% m/m serious) for wild-growing fungi and ≤1% m/m total (≤0.5% m/m serious) for cultivated fungi.

Addresses food additives for fungi: acetic, lactic, citric and ascorbic acids are listed and are generally not limited except that acetic acid may be used up to 20 g/kg in pickled fungi and lactic acid up to 5 g/kg (alone or combined) in sterilized fungi; limits for pickled/sterilized fungi are specified separately.
Non‑substantive heading identifying acetic acid as an additive (specific limits are given elsewhere in the additives section).
Indicates lactic acid is generally not limited, except as further specified elsewhere for pickled and sterilized fungi.
Section heading referencing citric acid in relation to pickled and sterilized fungi but contains no substantive provision or limit.
Section heading referencing ascorbic acid with no substantive provision included.
Specifies acetic acid limit of 20 g/kg for pickled fungi.
Permits lactic acid up to 5 g/kg, singly or in combination, in sterilized products.
Section heading referencing citric acid in connection with fungi but provides no substantive detail or limit.

Provides hygiene requirements and recommendations: products should be prepared and handled per the Codex General Principles and relevant codes, be free from objectionable matter to the extent possible, and when tested be free from microorganisms, parasites, or microbial substances that may hazard health, with cross-references to specific hygienic codes for dried, pasteurized (canned) and quick-frozen forms.

sections.section: 5.1 It  is  recommended that  the product covered by the provisions of this standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), 1  and other codes of practice recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission which are relevant to this product.
sections.summary: Recommends that products covered by this standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969) and other relevant Codex codes of practice.

sections.section: 5.2 To the extent possible in good manufacturing practice, the product shall be free from objectionable matter.
sections.summary: Requires that, to the extent possible under good manufacturing practice, the product be free from objectionable matter.

sections.section: 5.3 When tested by appropriate methods of sampling and examination, the product:
sections.summary: When tested by appropriate sampling and examination, the product must be free from microorganisms and parasites in amounts that may represent a hazard to health and must not contain substances originating from microorganisms at hazardous levels.

sections.section: 5.4 Products covered by this standard which are in the dried or dehydrated form should be prepared in accordance with the provisions of the Code of Hygienic Practice for Dehydrated Fruits and Vegetables including Edible Fungi (CXC 5-1971) 2  recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.
sections.summary: States that dried or dehydrated products should be prepared in accordance with the Code of Hygienic Practice for Dehydrated Fruits and Vegetables including Edible Fungi (CXC 5-1971).

sections.section: 5.5 Products covered by this standard which have been pasteurized in hermetically-sealed containers should be prepared in accordance with the Code of Hygienic Practice for Canned Fruit and Vegetable Products (CXC 21969) 3  recommended by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.
sections.summary: Requires that products pasteurized in hermetically sealed containers be prepared in accordance with the Code of Hygienic Practice for Canned Fruit and Vegetable Products (CXC 2-1969).

sections.section: 5.6 Products covered by this standard which are in the quick-frozen state should be prepared in accordance with the Code of Hygienic Practice for the Processing and Handling of Quick-Frozen Foods (CXC 8-1976).
sections.summary: Requires that products in the quick-frozen state be prepared in accordance with the Code of Hygienic Practice for the Processing and Handling of Quick-Frozen Foods (CXC 8-1976).

sections.section: 5.7 Products covered by this standard which do not fall within one of the categories at 5.4, 5.5 and 5.6 above, for example, fresh edible fungi, should be prepared in accordance with the relevant sections of the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969).
sections.summary: States that products not covered by the dehydrated, canned or quick-frozen provisions (e.g., fresh edible fungi) should be prepared according to the relevant sections of the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969).

Contains weights and measures requirements including a minimum fill rule (product and packing medium occupy not less than 90% of container water capacity) and provisions for minimum drained weight percentages by container size calculated on the basis of distilled water at 20°C.

Mandates minimum fill: the container shall be well filled and the product including packing medium must occupy not less than 90% of the container's water capacity, defined as the volume of distilled water at 20°C the sealed container will hold when completely filled.

Specifies minimum drained weight percentages calculated relative to the weight of distilled water at 20°C that the sealed container will hold, with specific percentage values provided for different pack types (e.g., regular packs 50% m/m, vinegar packs 53% m/m).

Requires packaging for fresh fungi to be perforated if needed for airflow, mandates maintaining products at a low temperature sufficient to preserve quality during transport/storage/distribution (allowing controlled thawing/repacking followed by quick-freezing), and warns that dried products, grits and powders must be protected from moisture and insect infestation.

Adds fungus-specific labelling provisions to the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods, requiring accurate designation of the product's nature, indication of processing method, common and scientific species names, use of the word "mixed" for multi-species products, statements where salted fungi or added stalks are involved, and a complete ingredient list except for dried fungi (with further provisions for non-retail containers).

Requires the name of the food to indicate its true nature; allows using customary common names in place of 'fungus/fungi' and requires the processing method (e.g., dried, sterilized, quick-frozen) to be stated on the label.

sections.section: 8.1.1 Products complying with the definitions and other requirements of this standard shall be appropriately designated to indicate their true nature.
sections.summary: Mandates appropriate product designation indicating true nature, permits replacing 'fungus/fungi' with customary genus/species common names, and requires the label to state the method of processing applied to the product.

sections.section: 8.1.2 In the case of fresh, dried, salted, quick-frozen, fermented, pickled and canned fungi, the common name of the species shall be stated in addition to the word "fungi".
sections.summary: For fresh, dried, salted, quick-frozen, fermented, pickled and canned fungi, the label must state the common name of the species in addition to the word "fungi" and must also include the scientific name of the species.

sections.section: 8.1.3 In the case of fungus products consisting of more than one species of fungus, the word "mixed" shall form part of the designation.
sections.summary: Fungus products containing more than one species must include the word "mixed" in the product designation and must state the names (including scientific names) of the species on the label.

sections.section: 8.1.4 In the case of fungus products made from fungi other than fresh fungi, there shall be a statement on the label indicating the method of processing to which the fungi used in the preparation of the final product have been subjected.
sections.summary: If a fungus product is made from fungi other than fresh fungi, the label must state the method of processing to which the fungi used in the preparation of the final product have been subjected.

sections.section: 8.1.5 Where salted fungi are used as raw material for the  preparation of other fungus products, there shall  be  a statement on the label indicating that salted fungi have been used.
sections.summary: When salted fungi are used as a raw material for preparing other fungus products, the label must indicate that salted fungi have been used.

sections.section: 8.1.6 If stalks have been added to fresh fungi or fungus products, the words "stalks added" shall appear on the label.
sections.summary: If stalks have been added to fresh fungi or fungus products, the label must include the words "stalks added."

sections.section: 8.2 Other styles - If the product is produced in accordance with the other styles provision (subsection 3.2.4), the label shall contain in close proximity to the name of the product such additional words or phrases that will avoid misleading or confusing the consumer.
sections.summary: Where a product is produced under the "other styles" provision (subsection 3.2.4), the label must include additional words or phrases near the product name to avoid misleading or confusing the consumer.
A complete list of ingredients shall be declared on the label in descending order of proportion, except for dried fungi (which are exempted).
Labelling of non-retail containers should be carried out in accordance with the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).
To check compliance with this standard, the methods of analysis and sampling in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) relevant to the standard's provisions shall be used.
